title: Erdős' question on $f_{3,k}$
statement_text: For each fixed integer $k > 3$, is the limit $f_{3,k} := \lim_{n\to\infty} \frac{\log f_{3,k}(n)}{\log n}$ always strictly less than $2$?
context_text: Erdős observed in 1973 that $\log f_{3,4}(n)/\log n > 1.4649$ holds for infinitely many $n$, noted that the limit $f_{3,k}$ exists for each fixed $k > 3$, and asked whether it is always less than $2$. Simmons and Abbott later showed $f_{3,4}(n) \geq n^{1.623}$ infinitely often and that $f_{3,k} \to 2$ as $k \to \infty$, but the question for fixed $k$ remained open.
paper_contribution: The paper answers Erdős' question in the negative by proving the more general Theorem 1.1: for all integers $k > s \geq 3$, $\lim_{n\to\infty} \log f_{s,k}(n)/\log n = 2$, i.e., $f_{3,k} = 2$ for every fixed $k > 3$.
paper_title: Sets without $k$-term progressions can have many shorter progressions
